events.title: Greek alphabet adoption and early writing
events.description: By the late 9th and early 8th centuries BCE, Greek city-states were adopting the Phoenician alphabet and adapting it to their own language. This process was gradual, occurring over several decades. By 797 BCE, early Greek inscriptions were beginning to appear, though the vast majority of Greek writing from this period remains oral. The adoption of writing was a crucial technological and cultural development that would enable the preservation of Greek literature and law.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Greek inscriptions and pottery
Historiographic consensus on Greek alphabet adoption

events.certainty_note: The adoption of the Greek alphabet from Phoenician sources is well-established archaeologically and occurred during the 8th century BCE. Specific events for 797 BCE are not documented; this entry describes the broader technological context.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Assyrian kings in this period is debated, with some scholars proposing alternative dating schemes based on astronomical calculations and king list variants. The exact timeline of Greek alphabet adoption is also contested, with some scholars placing it earlier (c. 800 BCE) and others later (c. 750 BCE). No disconfirming evidence specifically contradicts the events listed, but the dating precision is limited.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 797 BCE is minimal. No contemporary written records specifically document events of this year. The historical record relies on later compilations and archaeological evidence. Chronological uncertainty is significant, with margins of ±5–10 years common. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Mesopotamia and the Mediterranean; vast regions remain archaeologically silent or undocumented.

era_context: The early Iron Age Mediterranean and Near East. The Neo-Assyrian Empire is consolidating regional dominance. The Greek Archaic period is in its early phases. Phoenician maritime networks are at their height. In China, the Western Zhou dynasty is approaching its final collapse (traditionally dated to 771 BCE). Egypt remains fragmented.

events.title: Western Zhou dynasty in decline
events.description: The Western Zhou dynasty (c. 1046–771 BCE) was in its final decades. By 798 BCE, the central authority of the Zhou kings was weakening, with regional lords (zhuhou) accumulating power. The traditional account attributes this decline to the reign of King You (r. 781–771 BCE), whose policies allegedly alienated the nobility. Archaeological evidence from this period shows continued bronze-working and agricultural development, but political fragmentation is evident in the distribution of bronze vessels and inscriptions.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE
Bronze inscriptions (oracle bone script and bronzeware)
Archaeological evidence from Zhou sites

events.certainty_note: The Western Zhou collapse is traditionally dated to 771 BCE. The reign of King You is attested in later chronicles, but contemporary bronze inscriptions do not provide year-specific detail. The broader context of decline is archaeologically supported.
cross_references: Relates to -771-001 (traditional date of Western Zhou collapse)

events.title: Phoenician maritime expansion
events.description: Phoenician city-states (Tyre, Sidon, Byblos) were at the height of their maritime dominance in the 8th century BCE. By 798 BCE, Phoenician traders and colonists were establishing settlements throughout the Mediterranean, including in North Africa (Carthage's traditional founding date is 814 BCE, but Phoenician presence in the region predates this). Phoenician ships were the most advanced in the Mediterranean, and their alphabet was being adopted by Greek traders.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Phoenician settlements and shipwrecks
Greek historiography (Herodotus, Strabo) on Phoenician expansion
Phoenician inscriptions and epigraphic evidence

events.certainty_note: Phoenician maritime dominance in the 8th century BCE is well-established archaeologically. Specific events for 798 BCE are not documented; this entry describes the broader context of Phoenician expansion during this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional date of the Western Zhou collapse (771 BCE) is based on later chronicles and is not independently confirmed by contemporary sources. Some modern scholars argue for a more gradual decline rather than a sudden collapse. The exact dating of King You's reign and the circumstances of the Zhou collapse remain debated. Phoenician settlement chronologies in the western Mediterranean are also contested, with some sites showing earlier or later occupation than traditionally assumed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 798 BCE is minimal. No contemporary written records specifically document events of this year. The historical record relies on later compilations (Chinese chronicles, Greek historiography) and archaeological evidence. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and China; vast regions remain archaeologically silent. Chronological uncertainty is significant, with margins of ±5–10 years common for this period.

era_context: The early Iron Age Mediterranean and Near East. The Neo-Assyrian Empire under Sargon II's predecessors is consolidating power in Mesopotamia. The Greek Dark Ages are ending; the Archaic period is beginning with the emergence of the polis. Phoenician city-states dominate Mediterranean trade. In China, the Western Zhou dynasty is in its final decades before collapse. Egypt is fragmented under the late 21st Dynasty.

events.title: Sargon II's military campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Sargon II (r. 722–705 BCE) was likely conducting consolidation campaigns in the Levant during this period, though specific events for 799 BCE are not well-attested. The broader context of Assyrian expansion under his predecessors (particularly Shalmaneser III) set the stage for later conquest. This year falls within the period of Assyrian military reorganization and administrative expansion.

sources.name: Sargon II's annals and inscriptions (later compilation)

events.certainty_note: Sargon II's reign is well-dated (722–705 BCE), but specific events for 799 BCE are not independently attested. This entry reflects the broader military context of the era rather than a documented event for this specific year.

events.title: Greek Archaic period consolidation
events.description: By 799 BCE, the Greek Dark Ages (c. 1100–800 BCE) were ending. The polis system was beginning to emerge, with early forms of political organization developing in city-states like Athens, Corinth, and Sparta. Alphabet adoption from Phoenician sources was underway. This period saw the composition of early Greek epic poetry, though the Homeric epics were likely still being orally transmitted.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Greek settlements (pottery, architecture)
Historiographic consensus on Greek Archaic chronology

events.certainty_note: The broader Archaic period (c. 800–480 BCE) is well-established archaeologically. Specific events for 799 BCE are not documented; this entry describes the era context.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events for 799 BCE have disconfirming evidence. However, the dating of the Greek Archaic period's beginning is debated: some scholars place it closer to 750 BCE, while others extend it back to 800 BCE. The exact chronology of early Assyrian campaigns under Sargon II's predecessors remains contested due to overlapping king lists and variant chronologies in cuneiform sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 799 BCE is minimal. No contemporary written records specifically document events of this year. The historical record is dominated by later compilations (Assyrian annals, Greek historiography written centuries later). Archaeological evidence provides broad context but rarely yields year-specific data. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Mesopotamia and the Mediterranean; vast regions (Africa, Americas, most of Asia) are archaeologically silent for this period. Chronological systems for this era rely on king lists, astronomical calculations, and radiocarbon dating, all of which carry uncertainty margins of ±5–10 years or more.
